---
title: "1. Plan & Design"
description: "Explore existing resources, check legal requirements, write data management plans, design studies, and prepare preregistrations"
body-class: orc-phase-1
phase_order: 1
sidebar: open-research-cycle
page-layout: full
css: orc-styles.css
title-block-style: none   
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.0/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
---

<div class="orc-phase-hero-compact">
<div class="orc-hero-cycle phase-1">
{{< include ../images/os-cycle.svg >}}
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-body">
<div class="orc-hero-tagline">Set the foundation for open & reliable research</div>
<div class="orc-hero-pills">
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Explore & Reuse</span> 
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Check Legal Frameworks</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Write Data Management Plan</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Design Study</span>
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-checkpoint"><i class="bi bi-people-fill"></i> Team Checkpoint: Study Plan Presentation & Preregistration Submission</div>
</div>
</div>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary ><i class="bi bi-search"></i> <h4>1.1. Explore and Reuse</h4></summary> 

Any resource that inspire you or that you want to reuse and/or adapt must minimally be cited, using their DOIs (and otherwise URL with author, date, and time of access) and you must follow the license and/or usage agreement provided by the authors. 
A research output (e.g. data, code) without a license cannot be reused even if they appear publicly online.
<!-- this whit space seems kind of big? cut a third of it? apply everywhere-->

::: {.panel-tabset}
### 1.1.1. Articles <!--make this, and other tabs title everywhere, a shareable url?  -->

* To come up with a well-founded research question, **review existing literature** on open source registries like [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/). 
* Keep track of your bibliography with an open source **reference manager** like [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/). It can be integrated within RStudio to write reproducible manuscripts seamlessly, formatting your bibliography in any format you want (see [3. Analyze & Collaborate](03_analyze_and_collaborate.qmd){.mark}).

<!-- Cards should be now link to respective links on clicking anywhere in the cards-->
```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2"><img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f0/Zotero_logo_new.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="Zotero logo"></div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Introduction to Zotero</h4>
        <p class="card-text">An open-source reference manager that integrates with Word, RStudio, and Google Docs. (1h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <!-- TOOLS & RESOURCES Card -->
    <div class="col">
      <h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
      <a href="https://openalex.org/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg/250px-OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="OpenAlex icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">OpenAlex</h4>
        <p class="card-text">All the world's research, connected and open</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

### 1.1.2. Preregistrations

A **preregistration** typically consists of a hypothesis and predictions, a plan for data collection (when relevant), and a plan for data analysis, that researchers upload before starting their projects, often in order to increase the rigor of confirmatory research (see [1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning](#pre-analysis-planning){.mark}).
<!-- this cross ref is not working, it's not a md title FIXME either link to the study design ana analysis plan title or the tab pre analysis plan if possible. with an internal looking cross ref greyish thing-->

<br>

To have some **insight into projects that are not (yet) published**, either currently ongoing or abandoned, you can therefore look for projects that were preregistered. Projects that are left unpublished typically have a note attached to their preregistration. Some registries are discipline-specific while others are discipline-agnostic:

 <!--make a nice list or make them all clickable cards? maybe just OSF as card as this is the one we teach with? the ;website version has many cards - I don't even know them all - should check if correct (given ChatGPT) and add to list-->

```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://osf.io/registries/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="OSF icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">OSF</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Registry of preregistrations. Widely used across fields.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://aspredicted.org/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="AsPredicted icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">AsPredicted</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Registry of preregistrations. Widely used across fields.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://preclinicaltrials.eu/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://preclinicaltrials.eu/images/logo-pct.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="PreclinicalTrials.eu icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">PreclinicalTrials.eu</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Comprehensive listing of preclinical animal study protocols.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
        <div class="col">
      <a href="https://www.animalstudyregistry.org/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="animalstudyregistry.org icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">animalstudyregistry.org</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Online registry of animal studies across all disciplines.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
        <div class="col">
      <a href="https://clinicaltrials.gov/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
            <!-- need a better logo here-->
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a1/ClinicalTrials.gov-logo.png" class="img-fluid" alt="ClinicalTrials.gov icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">ClinicalTrials.gov</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Registry of clinical trial protocols.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

```


### 1.1.3. Data

To find **existing datasets**, search for discipline-specific repositories on [re3data](https://www.re3data.org/) which is a central registry of all repositories, or explore subject agnostic repositories such as [DataCite](https://datacite.org/) or [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) (oftentimes with the corresponding analysis code). These platforms either give you access to existing data or provide metadata and explanations on how to request access to the data. **Metadata** are data about your data, such as author, date, measurement device, unit of measurement, context of data collection, ect.

<br>

Found a dataset to reuse? Make sure you know where your data comes from, how the data was collected and processed, and reflect on whether any of it poses problems for your research question. Finally, check what requirements and resources the data sources have. Some requirement to access the data may be to submit a preregistration (see [1.4. Study Design & Analysis plan](#1-4-study-design-analysis-plan){.mark}). Either way, in order not to introduce confirmation or hindsight bias in your data analyses, do not start plotting the data itself but instead **familiarize yourself with the metadata to plan your analyses** (see [1.4. Study Design & Analysis plan](#1-4-study-design-analysis-plan){.mark}). Specifically, there may be a data dictionary (also called "codebook"), or other extensive documentation on which variables and their description are included. 

```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://www.re3data.org/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/fe/Re3data_Logo_RGB_72dpi.png" class="img-fluid" alt="re3data icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">re3data</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Registry of data repositories. Search across disciplines to find domain-specific options.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://www.re3data.org/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg/250px-OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg.png" class="img-fluid" alt="OpenAlex icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">re3data</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Registry of data repositories. Search across disciplines to find domain-specific options.</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://zenodo.org" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/zenodo" class="img-fluid" alt="Zenodo icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Zenodo</h4>
        <p class="card-text">General-purpose repository, DOIs</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```
<!-- add data cite and zenodo card as tool-->

### 1.1.4. Code

You may find **code available for reuse** archived on [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) or actively developed on [GitHub](https://github.com/). Learn how to work with GitHub in more details in [3. Analyze & Collaborate](l/03_analyze_and_collaborate_website.qmd){.mark} or start learning Git version control now! 
 
<br>

:::{.callout-important}
Code publicly visible on GitHub without a license cannot be legally reused. Ask the authors to add an **open license** to their repository to allow reuse (they must add a file called LICENSE.txt with e.g. the Apache 2.0 license text - see our [code publishing tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/code-publishing/choose_license.html) to learn more about licenses).
:::
<!--  add github tutorials in learn more, add github card as a tool - remove gitlab for now-->
<!-- comment RL: what does this mean ? two separate cards for GitHub? one under learn more and other within tools and resources? -->
<!-- add zenodo card in tool-->


```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
     <h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-RStudio-Git-GitHub/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-2"><img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/github" alt="GitHub" class="img-fluid" alt="GitHub logo"></div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">GitHub Tutorial</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Learn how to use version control system Git from within RStudio.(1h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-shield-check"></i><h4>1.2. Legal Requirements</h4></summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

### 1.2.1. LMU guidelines

The [LMU Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Scientific Practice](https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/media/contenthub/amtliche-veroeffentlichungen/gwp-ordnung.pdf) (in german) are legally binding for all academics, researchers, research support staff, teachers, and students at LMU Munich. Only the original text in German prevails, but here is an English summary of relevant aspects for this guide: 

<br>

<h5>Appropriate level of documentation and standards to allow **reproduction**:</h5> <!-- should probably not be h4 level but do some kind of nice formatting -->

* Reproducible methods must be used. (§11)
* When research software is developed, its source code must be documented. (§12)

<h5>Appropriate level of documentation and standards to allow **replication**:</h5>

* All information relevant to the production of a research result must be documented comprehensively to enable replication. (§7 and §12)
* If specific professional recommendations exist for review and evaluation, the results must be documented in accordance with these respective specifications. (§12) 
* Individual results that do not support the hypothesis must also be documented; a selection of results is not permitted. (§12)

<h5>Public access to research results:</h5>

* Apart from specific exceptions, **all findings should be made public**. For this, they must be described in a detailed and comprehensible manner which includes making available the research data, materials and information on which the results are based, as well as the methods used and the software employed (including appropriately licensed self-written software) **according to the FAIR principles**. (§13)
* Data, material, software made publicly accessible must be appropriately archived, usually for a period of 10 years (§17).

</em>

:::{.callout-note}

The **FAIR** principles are defined as:  

- **F**indable: metadata should be deposited in a searchable repository and be assigned a permanent identifier\
- **A**ccessible: the data is either open, or accessible upon some authentication process, or closed, but with open metadata.\
- **I**nteroperable: the data is described with a standard terminology (so the dataset can be merged with other ones) and saved in a stable file format\
- **R**eusable: the data is richly documented (e.g. with a data dictionary) and is accompanied by a data usage license

See [https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) for more information and section [2. Collect & Manage](/02_collect_and_manage.qmd){.mark} to learn how to implement the FAIR principles in your research.
:::

<!-- add a card with LMU logo and LMU Good Scientific Practices as title with link to pdf in section Resources
Comment RL: which pdf (instructions unclear)-->

```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="#" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">LMU Good Scientific Practices</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Guidelines...</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

In later sections, you will acquire skills in FAIR data management and reproducible workflows that will enable you to comply with these guidelines.

### 1.2.2. Funders

Funders may have additional **open and reproducible science requirements** on top of those indicated in the LMU guidelines. For instance, some calls request a Research Data Management plan before making their second payment, some specify the extent and timing of data sharing and provide funds for such activity. Check all requirements in the call information sheets.

<br>

**Contact the [LMU Research Funding Unit](https://www.lmu.de/en/research/research-services/contact.html) to review your grant proposal** and assess if your proposal is meeting your funders' open science requirements. 

### 1.2.3. Ethics

Data collection and analyses conducted on animals and humans typically requires the approval of your Faculty's ethics committee (see e.g. [instructions for the LMU Faculty of Medicine](https://www.med.lmu.de/de/fakultaet/wer-wir-sind/gremien-kommissionen/ethikkommission/) and [instructions for the LMU Department of Psychology](https://www.lmu.de/psyedu/en/research/ethics-committee/#:~:text=The%20Ethics%20Committee%20advises%20and,and%20in%20security%2Drelevant%20areas.)) to make sure your research will be conducted responsibly and that participants’ data will be protected.

<br>

#### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your ethics proposal will typically include information on: 

* **Data storage and retention** – outlining how data will be securely stored, backed up, and retained over time. This information can be extracted from a more detailed Research Data Management plan (see [1.3. Research Data Management Plans](#1-3-research-data-management-plans){.mark}).
* **Risks if the data were leaked** – identifying potential consequences for participants or the research project if confidentiality is breached.
* **Data anonymization** – describing procedures to remove or obscure personally identifiable information to protect participant privacy (see [2.3.2. Anonymization](/02_collect_and_manage.qmd#2-3-2-anonymization){.mark} for options).
* **Language of the informed consent forms** – ensuring that participants clearly understand the purpose, procedures, and any potential risks of the study. Conditions for sharing their data should be clearly explained here.
* **Power analysis** to justify sample size – providing a statistical rationale for the number of participants, which supports the validity and ethical justification of the study. This, and more detailed information on the statistical plan, can be extracted from your pre-analysis plan (see [1.3.1. Pre-analysis planning](#1-3-1-pre-analysis-planning){.mark}).

<!-- learn more: add cards for RDM plan chapter in FAIR data management tutorial - 

Comment RL- isn't this card similar to what we have in 1.3.?

, data anonymization tutorial tba, power analyses tutorial-->
<!-- resources: add example consent form text from Felix?? or other resources e.g. https://www.ninds.nih.gov/sites/default/files/migrate-documents/sample_data_sharing_consent_language_rz_corrected_508c.pdf? https://ukdataservice.ac.uk/learning-hub/research-data-management/ethical-issues/consent-for-data-sharing/ ?-->

```{=html}
<div class="container my-4 gap-3">
 <h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">RDM plan</h4>
        <p class="card-text">An open-source reference manager that integrates with Word, RStudio, and Google Docs. (1h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial(tba)</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Data Anonymization</h4>
        <p class="card-text">An open-source reference manager that integrates with Word, RStudio, and Google Docs. (1h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Power analysis</h4>
        <p class="card-text">An open-source reference manager that integrates with Word, RStudio, and Google Docs. (1h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-folder2-open"></i><h4>1.3. Research Data Management Plans</h4></summary>

A Data Management Plan (DMP) documents how you will handle research data throughout your project. Writing a DMP prompts you to think and document decisions you might otherwise leave implicit.

#### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your DMP will ask:

- **What data** will you collect or generate (types, formats, volume, sources)?
- **How will you describe it** (metadata standards, documentation practices)?
- **How will you organize files** (naming conventions, folder structure, versioning)?
- **Where will you store it** (locations, backups, access controls)?
- **How will you ensure quality** (validation checks, error-handling)?
- **How will you share outputs** (repositories, licenses, embargo periods)?
- **What constraints apply** (consent, anonymization, GDPR, data use agreements)?

The specific questions vary by discipline, data type, and funder requirements. DMP tools like [RDMO](https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/) guide you through the relevant questions with funder-specific templates. Your DMP is a living document: start with what you know, and refine the details as your project develops (see [2.2. Data Management](/02_collect_and_manage.qmd#2-2-data-management){.mark}).

```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">

  <!-- Row 1: LEARN MORE -->
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4 mb-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
            <img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
          </div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">FAIR Data Management</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Includes a chapter on writing DMPs (2h total)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- Row 2: TOOLS & RESOURCES -->
  <h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://riojournal.com/browse_journal_articles.php?form_name=filter_articles&selfurl=&backurl=&sortby=0&journal_id=17&search_hidden=&search_in_=0&search_in_hidden=&alerts_subject_cats=&alerts_sd498444d0a_publishing_status=&section_type%5B%5D=231" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://riojournal.com/i/journal_logos/RIO-logo-black.svg" class="img-fluid" alt="RIOjournal icon">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">RIOjournal</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Examples of DMPs by discipline</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://rdmorganiser.github.io/img/logos/RDMO-logo.png" class="img-fluid" alt="RDMO logo">
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">RDMO</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Funder-compliant templates (DFG, ERC, Horizon Europe)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>

</div>

```

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-clipboard-check"></i><h4>1.4. Study Design & Analysis Plan</h4></summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

### 1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning 

Why should you plan your statistical plan prior to collecting data? 

<br>

Humans are prone to cognitive biases such as **confirmation bias** (seeking information that supports existing beliefs) and **hindsight bias** (believing outcomes were predictable after the fact). In research, these biases can distort findings, especially when researchers make analytic decisions after seeing results. Although statistical testing typically accepts a 5% false positive rate, **"researcher degrees of freedom"** — choices about data collection, exclusions, transformations, sample size, covariates, etc. — can dramatically inflate false positives when decisions are made post hoc. Practices like increasing sample size until reaching statistical significance, selectively removing outliers, or trying multiple analytic strategies **increase the likelihood of false-positive results**, often unintentionally.

<br>

The core problem is that analyses guided by observed outcomes allow biases to influence decisions, making many reported effects unreliable. A key **remedy is transparency and preregistration**: specifying hypotheses, methods, and analysis plans before data collection or analysis. Preregistration constrains bias in confirmatory testing while still allowing exploratory analyses, clearly distinguishing robust hypothesis tests from hypothesis-generating work. This improves credibility, limits false positives, and often leads to better study design through **early methodological feedback**.

<br>

You will benefit from using a preregistration template for: 

- experimental studies (i.a. with a manipulated variable): it will define what will be your confirmatory analysis and strengthens your claim

- observational or exploratory studies: it will help you move along the exploratory-confirmatory continuum

- qualitative studies: it will provide a way to document e.g. your positionality towards a subject in the course of a project.


Several [preregistration templates](https://help.osf.io/article/330-welcome-to-registrations) exist. While the [standard OSF preregistration template](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1gkN0Jp6Gu7GIA4Ne4YCDZ61nCLQRgt32moRdUg9AnVg/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.fwbi14d4b65g) is most commonly used, some are tailored for specific field or specific methods (e.g. systematic review, qualitative work, secondary data analysis).

<br>

#### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your preregistration will define your study's:

- **Hypothesis and predictions**
- **Data collection procedures**
- **Sample size and stopping rule**
- **Variables** (manipulated, measured, indices)
- **Statistical method** (model, dependent and independent variables, covariables, transformations)
- **Data exclusion criteria**
- **How to deal with missing data**

A great tool to create your statistical plan, especially for early career researchers still learning statistics and needing feedback from supervisors, collaborators, or statisticians on their design, is to **simulate data and write the possible statistical test to analyze that data** (see [1.3.2. Simulation of data](#){.mark} and [1.3.3. Power analyses](#){.mark}). 

<br>

Once completed, you can submit your preregistration on e.g. the [Open Science Framework (OSF)](https://osf.io/) before collecting or analyzing existing data. The plan can be **embargoed** (i.e. kept private for a predetermine amount of time, and for a maximum of 4 years of the OSF) to prevent scooping, then later made public upon publication. This process improves transparency and is inviting valuable **early feedback from collaborators**. An even stronger approach is **submitting preregistrations directly to journals (then called "Registered Reports")**, enabling peer review at a stage where methodological adjustments are still possible.

<!-- in a call out info box perhaps even mini-accordion?? -->
::: {.callout-tip}
## Registered Reports
Registered Reports are a publication format, now adopted by over 300 journals (see [participating journals](https://www.cos.io/initiatives/registered-reports)), where **studies are peer-reviewed before data collection**. Reviewers evaluate the hypotheses, methods, and planned analyses, allowing methodological improvements. If the plan is approved, the journal grants in-principle acceptance, meaning publication is guaranteed provided researchers follow the protocol.

<br>

After completing the study, authors add results and discussion sections, clearly separating preregistered confirmatory analyses from exploratory ones. Final review focuses on adherence to the approved plan and the validity of conclusions, not on whether results are significant. This model shifts **incentives toward asking important questions and using rigorous methods rather than chasing striking outcomes**.
:::

<!-- learn more: cards for a TBA preregistration tutorial --> 
<!-- Tools and resources: OSF card with real OSF icon used somewhere above linking out to https://help.osf.io/article/330-welcome-to-registrations + COS card linking to  https://www.cos.io/initiatives/registered-reports-->

```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">

  <!-- Row 1: LEARN MORE -->
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4 mb-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="#" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial(tba)</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
            <img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
          </div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Preregistration tutorial</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Step-by-step guide to creating preregistrations using OSF and other platforms. ()</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- Row 2: TOOLS & RESOURCES -->
  <h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://www.cos.io/initiatives/registered-reports" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/1/1d/Center_for_Open_Science.png" alt="COS icon" class="img-fluid" >
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Center for Open Science</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Provides preregistration templates, list of journals offering Registered Reports</p>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://help.osf.io/article/330-welcome-to-registrations" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
          <img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" alt="OSF icon" class="img-fluid" >
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">OSF</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Flexible templates, embargoes, file storage. Widely used across fields</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>

</div>

```


### 1.4.2. Simulation of Data

In our context, a computer simulation is the generation of artificial data to build up an understanding of real data and the statistical models we use to analyze them. You can simulate data to:

<br>

- **Test your statistical intuition or demonstrate mathematical properties you cannot easily anticipate**.  
_Example: Check whether there are more than 5% significant effects for a variable in a model when supposedly random data are generated._

- **Understand sampling theory and probability distributions or test whether you understand the underlying processes of your system.**  
_Example: See whether simulated data drawn from specific distributions is comparable to real data._

- **Perform power analyses.**  
_Example: Assess whether the sample size (within a simulation repetition) is high enough to detect a simulated effect in more than 80% of the cases. (see [1.3.3. Power analyses](#))_

- **Prepare a pre-analysis plan.**
_Example: To be confident about the (confirmatory) statistical analyses you want to commit to before data collection (e.g. through a preregistration or registered report), providing a simulated dataset to a statistician or mentor will allow them to provide concrete suggestions on the most appropriate statistical test to apply to your data. The code containing the analyses of simulated data can be submitted along with your preregistration or registered report for reviewers to exactly understand what analyses you intend to perform. Once you get your real data, you may simply plug them into this code and get the results of your confirmatory analyses immediately._

<br>

Generating an artificial dataset in R (see our [simulation tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-Simulations-in-R/)) is much easier than researchers often believe it to be and is helpful even when you need to make assumptions about variable distribution or when the parameter space is not well known.


<!-- card for simulation tutorial -->


```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">

  <!-- Row 1: LEARN MORE -->
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4 mb-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-Simulations-in-R/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
            <img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
          </div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Data Simulation in R</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Self-paced tutorial on conducting data simulations in R. (2h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

```


### 1.4.3. Power analyses 

Power analysis is relevant whether you are designing a project from scratch or running an analysis on already existing data. There are two main types of power analyses: 

<br>

#### A priori power analysis

To calculate the smallest sample size required to detect the smallest effect of interest, simulate data following our [advanced tutorial](https://lmu-osc.github.io/Simulations-for-Advanced-Power-Analyses/). 
For a very basic power calculation, you can use simple R function if you know 3 out of 4 of these parameters: 

- required sample size n (usually the one missing)
- desired power (default 0.80)
- the alpha level (default 0.05)
- the expected effect size (has to be estimated or extracted from the literature on the form of d, f, etc.)

To get support with pre-analysis planning, you can book a consultation with the [LMU statistical consulting unit](mailto:@stablab.stat.uni-muenchen.de) and visit the [StaBLab website](https://www.stat.lmu.de/stablab/de/) for more information. 

<br>

#### Post-hoc power analysis

Sometimes, you may not be able to control the sample size for your project (e.g., because data collection has ended and you only have a certain number of good quality samples). In this situation, you can compute a so-called “post-hoc power”. Beware: This power computation comes in two flavors - one is legitimate, and one is flawed and not defensible. 

<br>

The **legitimate post-hoc power** is computed with your actual n, and the same effect size that you plugged into your a-priori power analysis. This analysis gives you the achieved power to detect your assumed effect.

<br>

The **flawed version of post-hoc power** is called **“observed power”**: If an analysis yields a non-significant result, some researchers calculate the post-hoc power, but plug in the observed effect size. “Observed power”, however, is just a one‑to‑one function of the p‑value (a non-significant p-value returns a low power < 50 %, a just significant p-value of .05 always yields a power of exactly 50%). Observed power adds no new information to the p-value and is essentially meaningless. Do not compute this type of post-hoc power!

<!-- card for simulation for power analyses tutorial -->
```{=html}
<div class="container my-4">

  <!-- Row 1: LEARN MORE -->
<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>
  <div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-3 g-4 mb-4">
    <div class="col">
      <a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Simulations-for-Advanced-Power-Analyses/" class="card orc-card-tool h-100 text-decoration-none">
        <span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
        <div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
          <div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
            <img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
          </div>
        </div>
        <h4 class="card-title">Simulation for Power Analyses</h4>
        <p class="card-text">Guided tutorial on conducting advanced power analyses in R. (6h)</p>
      </a>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

```
:::


</details>

::: {.orc-gradient-box}

## <i class="bi bi-card-checklist"></i> Plan & Design Checklist 

To complete before presenting your complete study plan to your research group and, if applicable, submitting your ethics proposal and/or preregistration. Not all items are relevant for all fields of research or study type.
<!-- add a 'download a customizable checklist' button -->
<!-- prepare such downloadable file with checklist in word doc? + citation Ihle, Gupta, Schoenbrodt 2026 + licence CC BY SA in footnote + link to this webpage -->

<div class="row row-cols-1 row-cols-lg-2 g-4">
<div class="col-md-6">

**Background Information**

- [ ] Literature reviewed 
- [ ] Existing research outputs searched
- [ ] Funder's Open Science requirements identified
- [ ] Ethics committee instructions consulted

**Study Design**

- [ ] Research questions clearly defined
- [ ] Hypotheses and predictions specified
- [ ] Study design chosen and justified
- [ ] Variables and measures to collect identified
- [ ] Analysis plan prepared (with simulated data)
- [ ] Sample size determined (with power analysis)
- [ ] Inclusion/exclusion criteria defined
- [ ] Handling of missing data defined

</div>

<div class="col-md-6">

**Data Management Planning**

- [ ] Data storage and backup solutions documented
- [ ] File organization and naming conventions established
- [ ] Anonymization procedures defined
- [ ] Repository for data publishing selected

**Project Management**

- [ ] Team roles and responsibilities defined
- [ ] Resources needed for the project identified
- [ ] Timeline estimated and milestones defined

**Before Data Collection**

- [ ] Data Management Plan completed 
- [ ] Consent forms prepared
- [ ] Data collection protocols tested
- [ ] Preregistration submitted 
- [ ] Ethical approval obtained 

</div>
</div>
<div class="d-flex justify-content-center orc-checklist-download mt-4">
  <a href="#" class="btn btn-outline-secondary btn-sm">
    Download checklist <i class="bi bi-download"></i>
  </a>
</div>


:::
